sentences.text: My name is Henry Wellisch.
In Vienna, Austria.
Oh, September 22nd, 1922.
My father's name was Emil Wellisch, and my mother's name was your Jolan Wellisch.
Nee Deutsch.
No.
I am the only child.
Yes.
My mother had her sister and her family.
And on my father's side there was also his brother-- two brothers, actually.
And there were various other family members, cousins and second cousins.
So there was an extended family.
My father was essentially a small businessman-- small.
I would say small.
Apartment.
I would say yes.
Many Jewish people living there.
I'm not sure predominantly, but there were many Jewish people living there.
No, no.

They were dispersed, but the area that we were living in was what you might call a Jewish district, maybe can compare to the Bathurst and to this area here, you know Bathurst and Wilson or Bathurst and Lawrence area where many Jewish people live there but also many others.
Well, I went first to four years of elementary schools-- of elementary school then four years of what is call [GERMAN], which is a kind of a technical high school.
And then I left and I continued for another two years at a commercial high school.
Only after Hitler came, I worked as a-- as a cabinet maker's apprentice for about six or eight months.
Well, life was tough, I'll tell you, because I remember as a child starting around, let's say, in 1930s or so, there was a depression in Vienna.
And my father had serious financial difficulties.
I remember for instance, when I was a small child up to about 1930 or so, we had a maid.
And then around 1930 or '31 or so, my mother had to get rid of her maid, and we didn't have been a maid anymore.
And my father had a very elegant store in this one of the main fancy shopping streets of Vienna.
That store went-- it didn't go bankrupt but it didn't do well.
And my father had to give it up.
So for a while he didn't have anything.
And then he, after about two years of doing various things, he eventually started another business, I think in 1935 or so.
So it was a difficult time.
And it was also from-- there were also-- there was a lot of unrest in Vienna.
In 1934, there were two revolutions in Vienna.
In February there was a socialist revolution that had tried to overthrow the government.
And in July, there was a Nazi coup d'etat where the Nazis start to seize the government.
And I remember those things well because, you know, it was such extraordinary occurrences.
So I remember those two events very well.
Well, life-- while life was more or less normal, there was a lot of anti-Semitism in Vienna, you know?
And although, like I told you, I was not a-- we were not a religious family at all, but in retrospect, all my friends were Jews, you know, practically all of them, except one or two.
One of my colleagues in school was not Jewish.

But we were living in a Jewish area, and looking over my class in my high school class where I went to, to the high school where I went for four years, I think all of my colleagues, with the exception of one, were Jews.
So it was a Jewish area.
That gives you an idea how many-- how many Jews were living in the area.
Well since in my class, we were all Jews, we were all treated the same.
Most of the teachers were non-Jews, you see, most of the teachers.
Then when I went afterwards to the commercial high school, interestingly enough in the commercial high school, one would think they're mostly Jews.
They were not.
The Jews went into academics.
Well, I went to the commercial.
In my class of about 40, there were only three or four Jews.
And of course, before the Nazis came, we were treated equally.
There was a lot of anti-Semitism but, you know, this was not-- it was, you know, more or less normal, more or less.
The anti-Semitism showed itself that we simply moved in different circles.
You see what I mean?
As far as I remember, I had hardly any non-Jewish friends.
You see, all my friends were Jewish, you see?
So we didn't have any-- there was not, not that much contact, you see?

I remember, for instance, my father was told-- after Hitler came to power, my father was told by one over his business associates, not the associates, business acquaintances, he said to him, you know, Mr.
Wellisch, we have nothing against you.
[GERMAN] he said, to him, [GERMAN].
You are a Christian Jew.
It's the others that we don't like, the others you know what I mean?
The ones he meant probably ones from east, from the east, you know?
In Vienna, there was a lot of prejudice against the so-called [GERMAN], the Jews who came into Vienna from Galicia.
Even amongst the Jewish, even amongst the so-called Viennese Jews, they didn't like either, and amongst them were my parents too.
My parents didn't like the Jews who came from, from Galicia because these are the people who were-- who were some of them, when they arrived, they had those beards.
And even the Jewish people who were already completely assimilated, they thought that there are these new people coming in and they will bring the anti-Semitism because they look different.
They speak Yiddish.
And they're going around with those beards and so forth.
It's not good.
You see what I mean?
There was a lot of-- even my parents-- my parents never did like the people from [INAUDIBLE].
They never did like those people from Eastern Europe, Jewish people.
Oh, drastic.
It was like day and night.
There is one day in which I will never forget.
The Nazis arrived on March the 11, 1938 when, when the Austrian government kind of caved in and the German army moved in and occupied Austria.
But there was so much resentment against Jewish people in Vienna that there were kind of pogroms on the street, you know?
People had to scrub the sidewalk and terrible things.
Jews were beaten up and their furniture thrown out the window.
Terrible things were going on.
I can't-- nothing happened to us, I must tell you.
Nothing ever happened to me personally except that one time I was cornered on the street by a few people and they tried to beat me up.
But really nothing happened to me.
And my mother was accosted a few-- a few times on the street.
And told pig Jew, get out of here and stuff like that.
But let me tell you what happened to my father's business.
My father had at that time a business selling fodder for animals like chickens and horses and stuff.
Because there was still a lot of horse traffic in Vienna.
You know, the beer and ice was delivered by horse-drawn traffic in the '30s.
And many people have still had chickens you know, in the outskirts.
They had chicken farms and my father had a business supplying those people with grain and hay and so forth.
So at that time, in March, the German army still had a lot of horses.
So on March the 12th or the 13th, a few days after the Germans arrived, a German sergeant or something arrived at my father's store.
He came in and said, is this a Jewish store?
My father said yes.
So he said to him, look, we need all kinds of stuff for our horses and you have to deliver this and this and this and this all to this address at a certain barracks.
And you'll get paid for it.
And my father of course loaded it all up and he had it all delivered.
So after that, my father thought if that goes on like this, in about another week or so, all my, my store will be gone.
So what he did, he went to one of his Christian competitors and said, listen, I'm prepared to sell you my entire inventory at rock bottom-- at a discount.
You want to take it?
Well he said yes.
So my father sold him the whole stuff.
And he was out of business.
So my father was out of business within a week after the-- within a week after the Nazis had arrived.
So after that, we realized that we had to leave because there's no way staying-- no sense staying here.
You can't get another job or a business under those circumstances.
There were terrible things going on in Vienna.
You know, like, like I told you before, you could hardly go in the street.
I didn't go on the street anymore.
And I withdrew from school after a few weeks because in school, we had to sit on separate benches.
There were three or four Jews in the class.
We had to sit on a separate bench.
And every hour on the hour when the professor came in, he said, there was-- it had to.
It was obligatory.
The professor said, [GERMAN], and the whole class stood up and said, Adolph Hitler.
And we had to stand there and, of course, we didn't do that.
But we just stood there.
And it was just not possible to stay like that.
So I didn't even-- didn't even finish the school year.
By that time it was about April of 1938.
I didn't finish the school year.
I quit.
I never finished.
So that was-- that was the situation in Vienna.
And we, we tried to leave, of course, but there was no place to go.
That was the situation.
Oh, well for the first the few months at that time, my mother was still able to go shopping regularly.
But eventually, especially I think in 1939 as the war came closer, there were ration cards were introduced.
And Jews could only go shopping after certain hours, and there was not that much left.
You know what I mean?
But until that time, the situation was more, to a certain extent, normal.
There was-- my mother still had some stores where she had been shopping for many years, non-Jewish stores, because Jewish stores had been-- many of them went out of business.
And she had been shopping there for many years, so they, they treated her-- some of them, they treated her nicely.
She could still get enough food.
However, what happened was that many people ran out of money so the Jewish community introduced soup kitchens where people used go.
Now, we still had enough money because one of my uncles, who was a very wealthy man, had two or three apartment buildings and he was an Argentinian citizen.
He was not living in Vienna.
He was leaving in Argentina.
At the time, he was living in Switzerland.
And since he couldn't take the money anyway out, he-- the money that he collected from the rent, he gave to my father.
The money was given to us, so we had enough money.
However, to have enough money is not a good thing for people to know.
So as a result, my parents also went to the soup kitchen as if they had no money, you know, not to give that away.
Because there were all kinds of funny things going on, and it was better do it this way.
So we went once a day.
We went with whole pots and so forth and collected that-- the food from a certain soup kitchen.
You had to walk about half an hour to go to get to this place where the-- and that soup kitchen actually was supplied by the American-- by the Joint Distribution Committee.
I'm sure you've heard the name, The Joint.
It was called The Joint.
And who were these people?
What do you mean, who were these people?
Oh, it's the Joint Distribution Committee.
It was an American welfare organization, a Jewish welfare organization.
And they supplied the money, you know, as long as it was possible.
Well, you couldn't trust anybody.
There was days that-- of course, we had very little connection with non-Jews, but the less you talked about politics or whatever.
Your own priority first, the better, you know?
You didn't-- you kept to yourself and that was the best thing to do.
Well, you could be sent to concentration camp.
If you say, for like a stupid thing, let's say, the Nazis are gangsters and somebody overheard you on the street, you can get arrested and send to a concentration camp.
And some people did.
They thought they were still living in the past.
No.
Yes, when he arrived in Vienna, he was addressed the people-- they were-- there were at that time my understanding is half a million people assembled on the main square.
There was such enthusiasm when Hitler arrived in Vienna.
That was historic, you know?
And of course, as a result of that, he decided immediately to annex Austria, you see?
There was such enthusiasm.
Oh, I didn't go there.
Are you kidding?
I stayed away from the streets as little-- as much as possible.
People were beaten up here and there.
There was all kinds of sporadic violence.
There was sporadic violence.
I don't have to tell you about the one day which we shall never forget in my life, which was soon the 10th of November.
We used to-- we used to call that the 10th of November, but it's now known as the Kristallnacht.
We were living right next to a synagogue.
You know, like here on the next-- behind the wall there was a synagogue, like wall to wall.
So you know, on, on November 8th, 1938, there was this German counselor was shot in Paris by this-- by Grynszpan.
Grynszpan.
And we knew something terrible was going to happen.
And then he died.
He died.
He was wounded, and he died.
We knew something terrible was going to happen but nobody knew what was going to happen.
So on the 10th of November, like I told you, we were living in a Jewish district.
In our apartment house, I think there were all Jewish people.
You see, to give you an idea.
There was not non-- not-- except the concierge, or what do you call it?
The janitor was not Jewish.
And she was a very nice person.
She saved us.
Our janitor saved us.
Let me tell you-- tell you how about that happened.
So we were living, as I said, we were living right next door to a very nice synagogue.
On the 10th of November in the morning, we got up, and we were living on the third floor, on the top floor.
And from our living room window, we could overlook the roof of the synagogue.
I look out the window, and I see some soldiers walking on the roof, because the roof was a kind of flat roof with some cupolas on it.
I see some people are walking on it with steel helmets.
I still remember it.
They were walking around, and they were inspecting it.
And then they went.
And then it went boom and the roof collapsed.
And then of course, we heard screaming because you see we were living right next to the synagogue.
But on our side of street was the back of the synagogue.
The front of the synagogue was on the-- on another street.
So we could hear screams.
There was a big crowd apparently in front of the synagogue, and they had entered the synagogue, and they smashed everything up.
What happened was on our side of the street, our street was unfortunately closed.
The reason why it was closed was they were-- they were some doing-- they were doing some repairs to the sewers.
And they closed the street to car traffic.
And only pedestrians could get into the street.
And the street was partly dug up.
So there were no cars and all the crowds that were-- that were in front of the synagogue were on the other-- on the other street.
So what are we going to do?
My father said, you know what we're going to do.
This is a terrible Jewish district.
We're going to visit my wife's-- my wife-- my mother's sister who was living in a suburb, in a, a working class suburb.
And there are hardly any Jews living there.
So that's what we'll do.
So we sneaked out of the house.
And I think we took a taxi.
You know, there was-- ordinarily, you know, in the city nothing was going on, only in certain selected areas where there were synagogues and some Jewish districts.
But somehow we were able to get a taxi very easily, and we went out, which is quite far away.
It's like from here to, I don't know, a few miles away.
We arrive at my aunts' house.
They were also living in an apartment building.
We go up to the first floor and we knock on the door, or my father.
No answer.
No answer.
We knocked again.
No answer.
So we went down to see the janitor.
And the janitor-- by the way, my aunt was living in their own apartment building.
They owned the apartment building.
We go down to the janitor and she says, yes, they are home.
You are-- you are my mother's sister's husband and the son had been arrested.
And they're in the apartment, but they're not opening the door.
So we packed up our bags, well, we didn't pack our bags, but we left again.
So but where to should we go?
So my father said, you know, I remember I have an acquaintance.
He's a very big businessman or whatever he is.
And he's lives in a very elegant district, very elegant.
And he had said to my father, if ever something happens in your district, you come to us-- come to me.
You know, I'll look after you.
You come to us because we're living in heavily Jewish district.
So OK, so we packed-- we pack up our-- not our bags.
We, we collected ourselves and went to this place.
I don't remember, if we even went by streetcar.
But I don't remember anyway.
And we went to this other place.
We come there, and knock on the door.
There is no answer.
I look at the door and I see there is a paper, a piece-- a little piece of paper taped over the keyhole.
And I look at it and it says on it, [GERMAN], which is Gestapo.
So the place was already sealed.
In other words, he probably had been arrested and the place had been sealed.
So we left.
After we left, we went back again.
We arrived back at the house.
At your home.
Back at the home.
I don't remember at what hour.
We must have left about 8:00 or 9:00 in the morning.
And we arrived back, I don't know, several hours later.
I don't remember the exact time.
We arrived at home.
On our street in the middle of the street, there is a big pile of Torah scrolls and curtains and all stuff from the synagogue.
It was all burning.
A big fire, you know, all these Torah scrolls and prayer books, a big pile.
It was all burning.
But there was nobody there.
So these people had thrown all this stuff on the street.
It was burning.
So we knocked on the-- by the way, the apartment house door was locked.
So we rang the bell.
The janitor came.
She unlocked the door, and she let us in and she locked the door again.
And that was what saved us.
What she did was, she locked all the doors.
And then some gang-- some of those gangsters came and said-- that's what we heard afterwards.
They rang the bell and said, what's the-- are there Jews living here?
And she said-- she was a Christian.
She's said, there are no  Jews living in this building.
Go away, go.
Just jump in the lake.
So they went away, and she locked the door again.
So the door was locked all day long.
And that's-- nothing happened in our house.
That is not quite what happened to one of my cousins, who's all their furniture was thrown out the window, you know?
Have you ever heard of a thing like this?
Their furniture was thrown out the window.
You know, it's unbelievable.
Anyway so that was-- that was the 10th of November.
We used to always refer to it as the 10th of November.
So after, after the 20th of November all Jewish businesses had to close and all the stores were smashed and all the synagogues were burned and smashed.
It was just a disaster.
What street did you live on?
What was the name of your street?
[NON-ENGLISH]
Oh, my parents decided immediately it was time to leave when my father lost his business.
My father realized immediately.
But there was no place to go.
What did they do?
As I told you, for instance, my father had a brother who was a very wealthy Argentinian citizen.
Now, my father's brother had heard that you could go to Bolivia if you deposited 1,000 USD in a bank there for certain time.
So he did that.
He deposited-- you couldn't go to Argentina, by the way, because Argentina was closed.
OK?
Now, Bolivia, apparently, it was possible to go if you had the $1,000.
So, he deposited $1,000 in a Bolivian bank.
And we were supposed to go to Bolivia.
Then suddenly, the Bolivian government changed the law and that whole thing was cancelled.
So we didn't go to Bolivia.
In the meantime, one of my cousins who had made it to, to United States-- now that was-- that was about six months-- you know, that was-- the Nazi takeover was in March 1938.
One of my cousins immediately got somehow to the United States.
And she went to see a complete stranger.
And these particular stranger gave-- made out what is called an affidavit.
You know what an affidavit is?
OK.
I got an affidavit by this a Mr.
I think, Mr.
Kaplan from Boston.
I had all the documents.
I know how much money he had because he had to fill how much-- what you own and what you are.
I think it was a Mr.
Kaplan from Boston who gave this affidavit for me-- only for me, not for my parents because there was a difficulty with my parents.
As you know, the United States has a quota-- had a quota system.
The quota system is based on the place where you are born.
Now I was born in Vienna.
My mother was born in Austria.
My father, by accident, was born in what is now Poland, although he hated the Polish Jews so much.
He could never forget that he was born in Poland himself.
You know, that was-- he could never get over-- all his life he, who had hated the Polish Jews so much, he was himself a Polish Jew, you know?
[LAUGHS] But it was a pure accident because his family is from Hungary, but his grandfather at one time was residing for a short time there, so that's how he was born in Poland.
But the problem was because he was born in Poland, he, he fell under the Polish quota.
The Polish quota was over-fulfilled, fulfilled for about 10 years.
You see what I mean?
The Polish quota for to go to the United States was very small.
The Austrian quota, which was added to the German quota, was about 30,000 people a year.
So I could-- if I have an affadavit, I could go within a few months or my mother also.
But my mother wouldn't go without my father.
So we had to go somewhere else, but there was no-- no-- there was no place to go, absolutely no place to go.
So--
So we tried other things.
There was a question of going with a kinder transport to England, but I was a little bit too old, I think, for that.
Anyway I was on the list.
And then I was kicked off the list.
And then, then eventually the thing came from my-- my affidavit came, but then I had to wait.
You know, you get an affidavit.
Then you had to wait for your medical examination.
OK.
[LAUGHS]
